title: Frobenius Lifting and a Geometric Theory of Companion Forms
speaker: Vincent Pilloni
abstract: We describe the obstruction to lift Frobenius on certain Shimura varieties and apply this to the theory of companion forms for classical modular forms (Gross, Coleman-Voloch, Faltings-Jordan) and also in higher dimension.

proof_stages.label: Gross companion-form criterion and geometric target
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from Gross's modular-curve companion-form theorem, reviews why existing generalizations do not cover the desired irregular cases, and formulates the geometric goal of finding cohomology governed by the Hecke quotient obtained by killing the local extension class.

proof_stages.label: Cartier and Frobenius-lift obstruction formalism
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the de Rham complex in characteristic p, the Cartier isomorphism and Deligne--Illusie lifted inverse-Cartier maps, identifying the Hodge-filtered component of these maps with the obstruction to lifting Frobenius modulo p^2 and relating the resulting data to Fontaine--Laffaille comparison.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-variety factorization of the obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For Siegel Shimura varieties the Frobenius-lift obstruction is expressed using the Hasse invariant and the nonordinary locus Z, factoring through a map on Z whose conormal component is described by explicit Goldring--Koskivirta-type Hasse invariants; in the modular-curve case this completely describes the obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Overconvergent canonical-isogeny proof
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the factorization uses the canonical Frobenius lift and canonical isogeny on the ordinary locus, shows that they extend modulo p^2 to the overconvergent neighborhood obtained by adjoining p divided by the Hasse invariant, and reads the obstruction from the derivation term in the formula f maps to f^p plus p g(f)/Ha.

proof_stages.label: Modular-curve companion complex E
proof_stages.summary: Returning to modular curves, the obstruction description is converted into an extension complex E, equivalently glued by a Green map at supersingular points, whose cohomology embeds into the spaces of weight-two and weight p-1 forms and, for p=2, behaves as the geometric avatar of weight-one forms.

proof_stages.label: Galois comparison and Gross criterion recovered
proof_stages.summary: After localizing at an ordinary eigenform, the étale-cohomology exact sequence and its finite-flat extension class are identified with the localized cohomology of E, yielding the criterion that the local residual representation splits as 1 plus epsilon^{-1} exactly when the corresponding H^1(X,E) localization is nonzero, with prismatic arguments handling the p=2 boundary case.

proof_stages.label: Siegel threefold companion-form theorem
proof_stages.summary: The same obstruction-complex strategy is applied to the GSp4/Siegel-threefold setting: under p at least 5, irreducibility, a decomposed-generic auxiliary hypothesis, and vanishing of a specified local extension class, one obtains a companion form, with the proof relying on the determinant of the obstruction, a higher-dimensional Green map, and comparison-theoretic vanishing/injectivity inputs.

examples.name: Supersingular modular-curve Hasse section
examples.note: The speaker recalls that on the supersingular locus of a modular curve there is a modular form of weight p+1, whose (p-1)-st power is the familiar one-dimensional instance of the Hasse-invariant section appearing in the general obstruction description.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker identifies the supersingular-locus section as a weight p+1 modular form, takes its (p−1)st power, and uses it in the explicit obstruction/Green-map description for modular curves

passes.reason: It is the canonical Hasse-type section on the supersingular, i.e. basic/p-rank-zero extremal, modular-curve locus, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
the supersingular Hasse-type section lies on the deepest nonordinary/p-rank-zero locus and is used to describe the obstruction, not to refute a claim.
it is tied to the supersingular boundary/Newton-extreme locus and is used descriptively, not adversarially

examples.name: Characteristic-zero canonical-subgroup radius
